# Anatomy of Failure in Small Open-Weight Tool Agents: Quantization Effects, Reliability, and the Limits of a Deterministic Guardrail

*Dhruvil Shah — July 2026. Every number in this report is generated by
`analysis/analyze.py` from the raw episode logs in `runs/` (commit-pinned;
see `analysis/results.json` and `analysis/tables.md`). Reproduction:
README.md. Zero paid API calls were used anywhere in this project.*

## Abstract

Small open-weight language models (1–3B) are the only agents most people can
run for free, yet agent evaluations concentrate on frontier APIs. We build a
five-tool customer-support agent harness with a 25-task suite spanning five
probe slices (single-tool, multi-tool chains, distractor tools, underspecified
requests, injected tool faults), run 1,200 seeded episodes across a
model-size × quantization × mitigation matrix on free cloud GPUs, and grade
programmatically (blind-validated: κ = 0.86 success / 0.94 failure-category
before reconciliation). Three findings. **(1) Quantization dominates:** at
1.5B, Q8_0 more than doubles task success over the Q4_K_M build everyone
downloads by default (31.0% vs 15.0%; +16pp, Holm-corrected McNemar
p = 2×10⁻⁵) — an effect as large as doubling model size, and concentrated in
a qualitative shift of failure mode (the Q4 model often does not call tools
at all; the Q8 model calls them and fails later, at synthesis). This
contradicts the received view from static benchmarks that 4-bit quantization
costs only 1–3% on tool use. **(2) A deterministic verification-and-retry
guardrail — schema validation, typed errors, bounded retries — does not help
small models and can hurt:** −7.5pp at 1.5B-Q8 (p = 8×10⁻⁴, discordant pairs
16:1 against), n.s. changes elsewhere; most failures originate in tool
*selection* and answer *synthesis*, upstream of anything an argument-level
guardrail can reach. Yet the same guardrail *triples fault recovery* at 3B
(3/16 → 10/17 injected faults recovered), showing the mechanism works once a
model can act on error feedback. **(3) Reliability collapses under
repetition:** the best cell's pass^1 of 0.34 falls to 0.08 at pass^8; after
an unrecovered tool fault, 1.5B models recover 0/84 times and usually
hallucinate a concrete answer. The harness, data, and full logs are public;
the entire study reproduces in <2 GPU-hours on a free Kaggle tier or on any
consumer CPU.

## 1. Introduction

Position papers argue that small language models are the economically
rational substrate for agentic AI (Belcak et al., 2025). Reliability
evaluation, however, has concentrated on frontier API models: τ-bench
(Yao et al., 2024) showed even GPT-4o is inconsistent across repeated trials;
AgentBench (Liu et al., 2024) showed open-weight models lag; function-calling
leaderboards (BFCL) rank models by single-shot accuracy. What is missing is a
reliability-first account of the models people can actually run for free —
including the quantized builds they actually download — with failure
attribution and a test of the cheapest possible mitigation.

We ask: **how reliably do 1–3B open-weight models execute multi-step
tool-use tasks; where in the pipeline do they fail; does a deterministic
verification-and-retry guardrail close any of the gap, at what cost; and how
does quantization (Q4_K_M vs Q8_0) modulate all of the above?**
Pre-registered hypotheses (Phase 1 design doc, committed before any
experiment ran) and their outcomes:

- **H1** (pass^k ≪ pass^1): **supported** — best cell 0.34 → 0.08.
- **H2** (guardrail helps, concentrated on malformed arguments):
  **falsified** — no significant help anywhere in the core matrix; a
  significant *harm* at 1.5B-Q8.
- **H3** (Q4 underperforms Q8 at equal size, worse at 1.5B than 3B):
  **supported at 1.5B** (+16pp); the 3B quant pair ran as an extension
  (§5).
- **H4** (guardrail latency overhead <15% median): **supported** — paired
  overhead +0.16 s (+3.7%) and +322 tokens at 3B; ≈0 at 1.5B.

## 2. Closest prior work

**Self-Healing Agentic Orchestrators** (arXiv:2606.01416) contribute a
budgeted runtime-recovery *mechanism* reaching 98.8% success on a 100-task
fault-injection benchmark — but with an unnamed evaluation model, no
repeated-trial metrics, no confidence intervals, and call-count proxies
instead of wall-clock latency. We supply the complementary *measurement*:
named, reproducible small models at explicit quantization levels, pass^k with
CIs and paired tests, real latency, and a finding their abstraction could not
surface — that verification feedback can *reduce* success below a capability
threshold (§4.3).

**τ-bench** (arXiv:2406.12045) introduced pass^k on frontier API models with
an LLM user-simulator. We adopt pass^k, move the object of study to small
local models (an LLM user-simulator would violate our zero-cost constraint;
our clarifier is scripted), and add failure attribution plus a mitigation
experiment. **BFCL** (OpenReview 2GmDdhBdDk) defines schema-grading for
function calls, which we adopt, but ranks single-shot accuracy without
failure anatomy, repetition, or mitigations.

**ACBench** (arXiv:2505.19433) studied compression × agentic ability with
GPTQ/AWQ on static benchmarks and reported 1–3% tool-use degradation at
4-bit. We study the GGUF k-quants actually shipped by Ollama, in a live agent
loop with repeated trials, and find an order-of-magnitude larger effect at
1.5B — suggesting static single-shot benchmarks understate quantization
damage to agentic behavior. **TinyLLM** (arXiv:2511.22138) improves SLM
function calling by fine-tuning; our mitigation is inference-time and
model-free. **ToolMaze** (arXiv:2606.05806) and **AgentCE-Bench**
(arXiv:2604.06111) established tool-fault benchmarks (we reuse the
fault-injection idea as one slice); **PALADIN** (arXiv:2509.25238) trains
recovery behavior. **MAST** (arXiv:2503.13657) taxonomizes *multi-agent*
failures; our taxonomy is single-agent and pipeline-staged. Long-context
degradation (RULER; Lost-in-the-Middle; arXiv:2510.05381) is out of scope by
design. Clarification-seeking under ambiguity is an active area
(EMNLP 2025 "Learning to Ask"; arXiv:2511.08798; arXiv:2606.03135) — our S4
slice measures the behavior without proposing a method.

## 3. Method

**Agent.** A minimal ReAct-style loop (no framework) against Ollama's native
tool-calling API: system prompt → task → iterate tool calls → final answer,
max 10 model calls, temperature 0.7, top-p 0.9, seeds 1–8 per task,
`num_ctx` 4096. Every request, response, tool execution, and timing field is
logged per episode (JSON, atomic, resumable).

**Tools (5, all local and deterministic).** AST-whitelisted calculator;
BM25 search over an 64-document synthetic policy corpus; two parameterized
SQLite lookups (`get_order`, `find_products`) over a seeded 40-product /
30-order database; subprocess `run_python` (5 s, isolated). The world is
fictional ("Zephyra Outfitters"), so ungrounded answers cannot be quietly
correct via memorization.

**Tasks (25, five slices).** S1 single-tool (5), S2 multi-tool chains (8),
S3 distractor-tool (4), S4 underspecified with a scripted clarifier (4),
S5 injected tool faults — error / timeout / empty, firing once then clearing
(4). Gold answers are *resolved from the generated world at grading time*
(`grading/gold.py`), so data and answers cannot drift; S4 golds are
conditional on whether the agent asked.

**Grading and attribution — programmatic, no LLM judge.** Success =
normalized match of the extracted final answer (numeric tolerance, date, or
fuzzy string). First-failure attribution walks the event log:
`no_tool_call, wrong_tool, malformed_args, bad_arg_values,
ignored_tool_error, synthesis_error, max_turns`; plus S4 behavior
(asked / looked-up / guessed) and S5 recovery flags. **Validation:** 42
stratified episodes were hand-labeled *blind* (trajectories printed without
classifier output). Agreement before any reconciliation: 40/42 on both
success (Cohen's κ = 0.86) and failure category (κ = 0.94). Both
disagreements shared one root cause (a contraction, "delay'd", missed by
string normalization); after a one-line edit-distance fix the same sample
agrees 42/42 (in-sample, reported as such). Two grader defects found earlier
in the pilot (digits inside product names parsed as answers; word-numbers
unparsed) were fixed before the matrix and are logged in NOTES.md.

**Guardrail (mitigation under test).** Deterministic and model-free: JSON
schema validation before execution; violations return a typed error naming
the exact problem, with bounded retries (≤2 consecutive per tool, ≤4 extra
model calls per episode); tool runtime errors become structured
`{error_type, message, retriable}` objects. Baseline passes invalid calls to
the tool layer, which fails like a real API (terse strings). Schema validity
is *logged* in both conditions, so malformed-call rates are measurable
everywhere.

**Design and statistics.** Core matrix: {1.5B-Q4, 1.5B-Q8, 3B-Q4} ×
{baseline, guardrail} × 25 tasks × 8 seeds = 1,200 episodes
(qwen2.5-instruct GGUF builds; model digests in the run manifest).
Pre-registered contrasts, paired on (task, seed): guardrail effect within
each model×quant; Q8−Q4 at 1.5B; 3B−1.5B at Q4 — exact McNemar tests with
Holm correction, risk differences with cluster-bootstrap 95% CIs (10,000
resamples, clustered by task). Per-cell uncertainty: Wilson 95% intervals.
Reliability: pass^k (unbiased estimator over 8 seeds, averaged over tasks).
Episodes ran on a free Kaggle GPU (P100) in 55.8 minutes; the identical
harness runs on a consumer CPU (measured throughput in
`PHASE0_RESEARCH_FRAMING.md`).

## 4. Results (core matrix, N = 1,200)

### 4.1 Overall reliability

| Cell | Model | Condition | Success | 95% CI | pass^4 | pass^8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | 1.5B Q4_K_M | baseline | 15.0% | 10.7–20.6 | 0.05 | 0.00 |
| C2 | 1.5B Q4_K_M | guardrail | 12.5% | 8.6–17.8 | 0.03 | 0.00 |
| C3 | 1.5B Q8_0 | baseline | 31.0% | 25.0–37.7 | 0.08 | 0.04 |
| C4 | 1.5B Q8_0 | guardrail | 23.5% | 18.2–29.8 | 0.03 | 0.00 |
| C5 | 3B Q4_K_M | baseline | 30.5% | 24.5–37.2 | 0.14 | 0.08 |
| C6 | 3B Q4_K_M | guardrail | 33.5% | 27.3–40.3 | 0.15 | 0.08 |

No cell is remotely reliable. The pass^k decay (Fig. 2) is steep everywhere:
even the best cell would complete all 8 repetitions of a task only 8% of the
time. This extends τ-bench's frontier-model inconsistency result to the
small-model regime, where it is far more severe (H1 supported).

### 4.2 Quantization is not free (H3)

Q8_0 vs Q4_K_M at 1.5B (baseline): **+16.0pp** (95% cluster-bootstrap CI
+6.0 to +27.0; discordant pairs 40:8; Holm p = 2×10⁻⁵) — the same magnitude
as doubling parameters at fixed quant (3B-Q4 vs 1.5B-Q4: +15.5pp, CI +0.5 to
+31.0, p = 3.5×10⁻⁴). The failure anatomy (Fig. 3) shows this is a
*qualitative* change, not uniform improvement: the Q4 model's dominant
first-failure is `no_tool_call` (61/200 episodes — it answers from nothing,
often emitting pseudo-JSON or garbage), which nearly vanishes at Q8 (5/200);
Q8's failures move downstream to `synthesis_error` (68/200). Quantization
changes *how* the model fails, not just how often. On S4 ambiguity probes,
the Q8 build asks a clarifying question 44% of the time vs 22% for Q4.
Static compression benchmarks (ACBench: 1–3% tool-use drop at 4-bit) do not
predict this; agentic, repeated-trial evaluation appears necessary.

### 4.3 The guardrail does not help small models — and can hurt (H2 falsified)

| Contrast | RD | 95% CI | Discordants | Holm p |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| guardrail @1.5B-Q4 | −2.5pp | −9.5, +3.0 | 3:8 | 0.42 |
| guardrail @1.5B-Q8 | **−7.5pp** | −17.5, −0.5 | 1:16 | **8×10⁻⁴** |
| guardrail @3B-Q4 | +3.0pp | −4.0, +11.0 | 11:5 | 0.42 |

At 1.5B-Q8 the guardrail flipped 16 task-seed pairs from success to failure
and rescued 1. Mechanism, from the logs: most failures start upstream of
arguments — wrong tool, no tool, or bad synthesis — where schema validation
is inert; and when the guardrail does block a malformed call, the verbose
typed error often derails the small model into loops or fabrication instead
of repair (e.g., a model that invented an `order_by` argument re-sent it
eight times, alternating blocked and pass-through, until the turn budget
expired). First-failure counts corroborate: `malformed_args` as first
failure *rises* under the guardrail at 1.5B-Q8 (19 → 34) because retries
multiply the opportunities to fail at the same step.

**But the mechanism works when the model can use it.** On the S5 fault
slice at 3B, recovery after an injected fault goes from 3/16 (baseline,
bare-string errors) to **10/17** with typed `retriable: true` errors — and
`ignored_tool_error` as a first failure halves (11 → 5). The guardrail's
value is gated on a capability threshold that 1.5B models are below and 3B
models are near.

### 4.4 Error recovery is the starkest capability cliff

Across all four 1.5B cells, **zero of 84 fired faults were recovered** — no
retry, ever. Worse, after an unrecovered fault the models usually produced a
concrete answer anyway: at 1.5B-Q4 baseline, 22/25 fired faults ended in a
hallucinated answer (a fabricated status, price, or figure), versus 3
honest refusals. The guardrail's typed errors shift some hallucinations to
refusals at 1.5B (22 → 13 hallucinated) without enabling recovery — arguably
its only benefit at that scale, and a safety-relevant one.

### 4.5 Ambiguity handling (S4)

The 3B model almost never asks (6%) but looks the default up in policy docs
(31%); the 1.5B-Q8 asks (44–47%) but rarely looks up; 1.5B-Q4 mostly guesses
(78%). Distinct, quantization- and size-dependent strategies, all far from
reliable.

### 4.6 Cost of the guardrail (H4)

Paired overhead at 3B: +0.16 s/episode on the GPU (+3.7%), +322 tokens; at
1.5B: ≈0 (fewer than half a blocked call per episode on average). Derived
consumer-CPU episode times (token counts × Phase 0 measured laptop
throughput; *derived*, not measured): ≈50 s at 1.5B-Q4, ≈225–243 s at 3B-Q4.

## 5. Extension matrix (cross-family and scale, N = 1,200)

*(Exploratory — not pre-registered; same harness, tasks, seeds; N = 1,200.)*

| Cell | Model | Condition | Success | 95% CI |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| E1 | llama3.2-1B Q8_0 | baseline | 14.5% | 10.3–20.1 |
| E2 | llama3.2-1B Q8_0 | guardrail | 8.0% | 5.0–12.6 |
| E3 | qwen2.5-3B Q8_0 | baseline | 43.5% | 36.8–50.4 |
| E4 | qwen2.5-3B Q8_0 | guardrail | 42.0% | 35.4–48.9 |
| E5 | qwen2.5-7B Q4_K_M | baseline | 43.0% | 36.3–49.9 |
| E6 | qwen2.5-7B Q4_K_M | guardrail | 42.5% | 35.9–49.4 |

Four observations. **(1) The guardrail harm replicates in a second model
family**: llama-1B drops 6.5pp under the guardrail (discordants 15:2,
Holm p = 0.007), with a completely different failure fingerprint —
`malformed_args` dominates (86–97/200; the model habitually invents argument
names such as `city` for `location`), so this is not a Qwen quirk. Across
all five model×quant settings tested in this project, the guardrail never
significantly helped overall success. **(2) The quantization pattern
recurs at 3B**: Q8 baseline 43.5% vs Q4's 30.5% (+13pp, descriptive across
runs on identical tasks/seeds). **(3) Quantization trades against size**:
3B-Q8 (43.5%) ≈ 7B-Q4 (43.0%) — twice, since 1.5B-Q8 ≈ 3B-Q4 in the core
matrix. Under a memory budget, downloading the higher-precision smaller
model appears at least as good as the bigger 4-bit one. **(4) Native fault
recovery tracks model quality and explains the guardrail's one benefit**:
3B-Q8 recovers 15/24 injected faults with *no* guardrail (bare-string
errors), where 3B-Q4 managed 3/16 — the deficit the guardrail compensated
(§4.3). 7B-Q4 recovers only 8/23, again behind 3B-Q8. The 7B's residual
failures are almost purely `synthesis_error` (61/200) with near-zero
malformed or wrong-tool calls — as models improve, failures migrate to the
one stage no tool-layer guardrail can reach.

## 6. Limitations

- **Synthetic single-domain world.** 25 hand-authored tasks in one fictional
  retail domain; effects may differ elsewhere. Task-clustered bootstrap
  mitigates but cannot remove suite-composition sensitivity.
- **Per-cell Wilson CIs treat episodes as independent**, but episodes
  cluster by task; those intervals are optimistic. All *contrast* inference
  uses cluster bootstrap + McNemar, which respects the pairing.
- **The 1.5B-Q4 floor** (15%) leaves little room to detect guardrail effects
  there (documented before the matrix ran; NOTES.md pilot gate).
- **Grader ceiling.** Programmatic grading was blind-validated (κ 0.86/0.94,
  42 episodes) and post-fix agreement is in-sample; residual grader error is
  possible on unusual answer phrasings.
- **One guardrail design.** Different error wording, tighter budgets, or
  in-context repair examples might behave differently; we tested one
  deterministic, model-free point in that space.
- **Sampling determinism.** Ollama seeds make runs reproducible on the same
  build/hardware; bit-exact reproduction across GPUs is not guaranteed. All
  raw logs are committed, so every reported number is recomputable exactly.
- **Best-effort sandboxing** of `run_python` (subprocess isolation, no
  network by task design), documented rather than hardened.

## 7. Future work

Guardrail designs that act at the tool-*selection* layer (the actual
dominant failure); repair-example prompting vs typed errors; extending the
quantization axis to Q2/Q3 and activation-quantized builds; a long-context
degradation axis (our RQ3 candidate); validating the capability-threshold
hypothesis on 7–8B models across families.

## References (all verified during this project; see NOTES.md)

- Belcak et al., 2025. Small Language Models are the Future of Agentic AI. arXiv:2506.02153.
- Yao et al., 2024. τ-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains. arXiv:2406.12045.
- Liu et al., 2024. AgentBench: Evaluating LLMs as Agents. ICLR 2024, arXiv:2308.03688.
- Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard (BFCL): From Tool Use to Agentic Evaluation. OpenReview 2GmDdhBdDk.
- Anonymous/verified 2026. Self-Healing Agentic Orchestrators for Reliable Tool-Augmented LLM Systems. arXiv:2606.01416.
- Dong et al., 2025. Can Compressed LLMs Truly Act? (ACBench). arXiv:2505.19433.
- TinyLLM: Evaluation and Optimization of SLMs for Agentic Tasks on Edge Devices. arXiv:2511.22138.
- ToolMaze: Benchmarking Dynamic Replanning and Anomaly Recovery in LLM Agents. arXiv:2606.05806.
- AgentCE-Bench. arXiv:2604.06111. — PALADIN. arXiv:2509.25238.
- Cemri et al., 2025. Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? (MAST). arXiv:2503.13657, NeurIPS 2025 D&B.
- Ruan et al., 2024. ToolEmu: Identifying the Risks of LM Agents with an LM-Emulated Sandbox. ICLR 2024, arXiv:2309.15817.
- Learning to Ask: When LLM Agents Meet Unclear Instruction. EMNLP 2025.
- Context-length degradation: RULER; Lost in the Middle; arXiv:2510.05381 (scope framing only).
